commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a Slack `allowBotsFrom` config allowlist for specific Slack `bot_id`s, with config schema/help/type changes, inbound bot-message filtering, and unit tests.

PR surface: Source +36, Tests +74. Total +110 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the patch defects: source inspection of the PR head shows type/test-scope/schema problems without needing to run the test suite. The live Slack feature behavior itself is not proven because the PR has no real workspace proof.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] This needs maintainer review for the new Slack config/security boundary and contributor-provided real behavior proof; it is not a safe automated repair candidate while the branch is dirty and the product direction is unsettled.

target.title: feat(slack): add allowBotsFrom for trusted partner-agent bot allowlisting
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a new `allowBotsFrom` config option (string array of Slack `bot_id`s) that lets specific trusted bots trigger replies even when the global `allowBots` flag is `false`.

## Motivation

Closes #89043 (partial — addresses the cross-app trusted-bot use case).

Multi-agent deployments — for example two OpenClaw instances that share the same Slack workspace but use **different** Slack app tokens — currently have no way to receive each other's messages without opening the gateway to _all_ bot traffic via `allowBots: true`.

`allowBotsFrom` fills this gap with a minimal-privilege approach:

```jsonc
// openclaw.json
"channels": {
  "slack": {
    "allowBots": false,             // still block unknown bots
    "allowBotsFrom": ["B012PARTNER"] // only this partner-agent bot gets through
  }
}
```

## Behaviour

| Condition | Result |
|-----------|--------|
| `allowBots: true` | All bots allowed (existing behaviour, `allowBotsFrom` ignored) |
| `allowBots: false`, `bot_id` in `allowBotsFrom` | **Allowed** (new) |
| `allowBots: false`, `bot_id` NOT in `allowBotsFrom` | Dropped (existing behaviour) |

List merging is additive: top-level, account-level, and channel-level `allowBotsFrom` entries are all honoured, enabling fine-grained per-channel control.

A verbose-mode log line distinguishes allowlist matches from ordinary drops.

## Files changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.slack.ts` | Add `allowBotsFrom?: string[]` to channel and top-level Slack config types |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` | Zod validation for new field in all three Slack config shapes |
| `src/config/schema.help.ts` | Help text / docs hint for the new key |
| `src/slack/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` | Merge lists and check `bot_id` membership before dropping |
| `src/slack/monitor/message-handler/prepare.test.ts` | Unit tests: drop, allow, merge, `allowBots=true` passthrough |

## Note on same-app agents (issue #89043)

This PR handles the **cross-app** case (two separate Slack app tokens). The **same-app** case (two agents sharing one Slack app token) requires a separate gateway-level internal-echo mechanism, as Slack's Socket Mode never re-delivers an app's own posts back to itself. That path is left for a follow-up.
target.author: niceysam
